g.data-narration: Welcome to Programmable Pipelines - the foundation of modern GPU rendering that revolutionized graphics programming.
A programmable pipeline replaces fixed hardware operations with customizable shaders, giving developers unprecedented control over rendering.
The journey from fixed-function to programmable began in 2001 with GeForce 3, enabling vertex and pixel shaders for custom effects.
The modern graphics pipeline flows from input assembly through vertex processing, rasterization, fragment shading, and finally output merging.
The Input Assembler collects vertices from memory, and the Vertex Shader transforms each vertex through model-view-projection matrices.
Tessellation dynamically subdivides primitives for detail, while Geometry Shaders can create or destroy entire primitives for effects.
g.text: Processes entire primitives → can add/delete geometry
Uses: Billboard particles, shadow volumes, wireframe

g.data-narration: The Rasterizer converts primitives into fragments, interpolating vertex attributes across the surface for per-fragment processing.
The Fragment Shader computes final pixel colors through per-fragment operations like texture sampling and per-pixel lighting calculations.
The Output Merger combines fragment colors with the framebuffer through depth testing, stencil operations, and blending.
Here's a vertex shader that transforms positions through model-view-projection matrices and passes data to the fragment shader.
g.text: // Transform position: Model → World → View → Clip
vNormal = normalMatrix * aNormal; // Pass to fragment shader
Output: gl_Position, varyings → interpolated → fragment shader

g.data-narration: This fragment shader implements Blinn-Phong lighting with ambient, diffuse, and specular components computed per-pixel for smooth results.
g.text: in vec3 vNormal; in vec3 vFragPos;
uniform vec3 lightPos, viewPos, lightColor, objColor;

g.data-narration: Texture sampling in fragment shaders fetches color values using interpolated texture coordinates for detailed surface materials.
g.text: in vec2 vTexCoord; // Interpolated from vertex

g.data-narration: Uniform variables pass data from the application to shaders, remaining constant across all vertices and fragments during a draw call.
